sentences.text: Yes.
Well, we were-- they took us to shul.
And then they gave most of these streets, Jewish people lived.
A matter of fact, they had there, when my father was teaching, also, the cheder.
And they gave us like one room to-- to a family or sometimes just a kitchen or sometimes just a foyer.
And we were-- my mother had two sisters.
And we were there, five children with my father.
And my aunts was there with their children.
One aunt had five children.
Her husband wasn't home.
He was also in the-- not in the army, in the forced labor camp.
And my other uncle, he was such a religious man, also ordained as a rabbi.
And he was there with his six children, with his wife.
So we hardly had a place to sit, not to sleep.
Like, people would have to stand up, the other kids.
And the-- there were little kids.
And some of them were sick.
And they cried all night.
And it was so crowded.
And we still had some food left from home that we brought, from home, food.
And we stayed in this place for three weeks.
It was very difficult.
But I-- I remember it was still, the water was very cold yet.
There was a river.
And [INAUDIBLE] was standing end of the block.
But they didn't guard the river.
So some boys went across the river.
And they left the ghetto.
And some of the peasants, they were nice people.
They came swimming across the river and brought us some food.
So a matter of fact, we left our cows there, my papa's bicycle.
And-- and they came.
They brought us some milk and bread and some cooked food.
But like, potatoes and hard-boiled eggs and potatoes and the peels.
They knew, already, what we could have, the peasants.
So-- but it was very, very difficult.
That-- we just were already so-- where we going to go?
Where they going to take us from here?
We knew that this is just like a station.
Because they brought from the little towns into this town, into this ghetto.
And we stayed there for three weeks.
And then after three weeks, they took us to the station.
And they put us in-- in cattle cars.
And we went to Munkacs.
To eat?
No.
Just what we-- we took alone.
And some people that lived there, they had some food that they shared with us.
But the [INAUDIBLE], they-- they didn't give us nothing.
Very bad.
Very bad.
It was-- we were already not washing, except the-- the river.
We didn't-- we couldn't heat the water.
So we would bring water from the river and leave it in the room.
It should be room temperature so we could wash ourself a little bit and change our clothes.
We still had clothes from home that we-- we tried to change and wash it in cold water.
But it was-- we didn't have lice yet.
But it was already-- we felt very bad.
It-- it was very difficult.
And then we have no privacy at all, because we were so many people.
And the people were very irritated.
You know?
And kids were sleeping, crying.
And if somebody was crying, woke up the next baby.
And so it was very, very difficult.
Yes, yes, yes.
Yes.
Yes.
It came shabbos.
The people went to synagogue.
The-- the men went to that big synagogue to daven in the morning and at night.
And the Saturday, they also went to daven there.
But we didn't have any Saturday meals no more.
We didn't have a table to sit.
We sat, most of the time, on the floor.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
We were sitting outside, you know, got dressed.
That was the best-- the best place for the young boys and girls.
You know?
We got dressed and walked outside, were sitting outside.
Because the rooms were so crowded with the babies and the old people.
So we teenagers would get dressed and go outside and sit outside or walk, walk from place to place, in the ghetto.
You know, in those few blocks, we would just walk.
No.
I don't think so.
I-- I wasn't-- I had my uncle that was-- lived in this town.
He worked in the mill.
He-- he came.
They came every day.
And they would take him out to work to the mill.
And they would bring him back.
And he used to bring some flour.
So we would cook, make something to eat, cook something, make noodles out of it and something to eat.
My aunt would share with us.
And it was very hard there.
We were hungry most of the time, hungry and depressed and scared.
Because we didn't know where we're going to go next, what's waiting for us.
And I remembered this cousin of mine, that my uncle that worked in the mill, his son.
He came back from-- they let him go home from the working camp.
So he came to the ghetto.
So they said, he could go to-- back to the army.
Or, he could stay there.
So my uncle said, let him go back to the army.
He has a better chance to the working camp.
And because they had another son that was also about 20 years.
He's going to be with-- with us.
If he has to take care-- because they had younger children.
So he was the oldest one, 20 years.
And the other one was 22, the one that they found in Serbia after.
So he-- he went to the army.
And a lot of boys at that time, they-- they went along to the working.
They had a choice to where they want to go.
You mentioned earlier the ride to Munkacs.
What do you remember about the ride and the-- the assembly?
Well, when they put us on the train, we didn't know where we're going.
So every time that there was a-- that-- that we would pray, we got together with my father.
And we prayed.
And we cried.
And we-- I remember my father took us together.
And he says, we don't know where we're going.
But there is-- we always could pray and HaShem will help us.
And we had-- there wasn't much of choices.
But so we prayed.
And we cried.
And we would ask questions, where, what?
But nobody was able to tell us where we going.
There was nobody to ask.
I-- I would ask my father.
But my father didn't-- didn't know more than I know where we going.
It was, like, a day maybe.
During the-- we left in the morning.
And maybe around 3 o'clock in the afternoon we were in Munkacs.
No, no, no.
We didn't know where we were going.
The condition, it was a cattle car.
And it was not too crowded.
We-- a certain amount of people went into the cattle car.
We were able to sit on the cattle car and cried and-- and prayed.
And-- and it was one day.
We were, of course, hungry and thirsty.
We didn't have any water.
We didn't have any food.
So we thought maybe somebody will give us food.
There were-- there was nothing on the train, nothing.
To the Irsava ghetto, [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], and [INAUDIBLE], and [INAUDIBLE] and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what other?
[INAUDIBLE], little towns from all-- all there.
They brought us into the Irsava ghetto.
And then they took these little ghettos.
And they brought to the Munkacs ghetto.
This was already a big ghetto.
Well, the-- we-- this was-- when we came there, we knew that we are at Munkacs.
But the-- the ghetto itself was, like, in the suburbs.
It was a brick factory.
They were making bricks there before.
And they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before we went into the ghetto, it was-- we were maybe two or three days outside.
Water, we had.
Because they had their, like, ditches, where they took the Earth for to make the-- the bricks.
So there was water.
So we drank that water.
It wasn't so clean.
But we had water.
But we have no food.
Some people still had, left from home, some food.
And the children were crying.
And the-- there, we saw the Germans.
And the Germans were hitting, especially the male.
And if somebody had a beard, they just took the beard and tore them off.
The [INAUDIBLE], the one that slaughter's the-- they tore off his beard with part of his skin.
And he was bleeding.
And we saw that.
[SOBBING] And we said, to our father, you have to cut your beard.
And my father says, no I won't do it.
And we start to cry.
I says, you have to.
You have to.
So somebody-- most of the people cut their beards.
Because they saw what was going on.
So my father, the first time I saw him without a beard, [SOBBING] they cut his beard.
And he took a kerchief.
And he put it on like he would have a toothache.
He covered, because he couldn't stand himself either without a beard.
And we were so hysterical when they cut his beard.
Because we know how much it meant to my father, how religious he was.
So they took all of us, like, about 100 people.
And they took them into the ghetto.
And when we-- we came in, finally, after maybe a day and a half or something waiting outside, we came in there with my aunts.
And there was, already, my aunt from Irsava, also, that he worked in the mill.
She was there too.
And they took this man that had the mill.
I don't know.
For some reason, they-- they knew who has the money.
They said, you have to give all the money and all the jewelry.
And they took about 10 boys, nice-looking boys.
And they took this man also.
And they undressed them half up to their waist.
And they put them against the wall.
And they said, if you're not going to give all the money and all the jewelry, we're-- we're going to kill these people.
And there was one boy, my aunt's boy, [?
Moishe. ?] He was a very handsome boy, tall.
And he was standing against the wall.
[SOBBING] And we cried.
We screamed.
Please, give them the money.
Give them the jewelry.
Who need it, already, anything?
The people gave them money, the jewelry.
Everything, they gave.
And they were hitting this man.
He was such a distinguished man, such a rich man that had the mill.
They were hitting him with the-- with the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had, like, clubs.
They were hitting the boys.
And they were screaming.
[CRYING] Everybody gave-- I thought everybody gave them money.
And then, OK.
So they let us already go.
And they said, take your bundles and go.
So there was like a-- a big building where they kept the equipment for-- for the factory.
So they said, the children and the women could go in there, in that place, in that big building.
And who doesn't have small children has to-- to build huts for themselves.
If not, you could sleep on the floor.
So we took bricks.
My father was always the one that helped to organize.
So we took bricks.
And we-- against that big building, we made two walls.
We made one wall this way and one wall this way.
And we covered the top with blankets.
Because it was still very cold after Pesach.
We were about two weeks in [NON-ENGLISH].
So he-- he-- we covered.
And the floor was still frozen.
It was muddy and frozen.
So we took, like, wood that they use to dry the bricks.
So we took that wood and put it on the floor.
And we were glad we had blankets.
We covered ourselves with blankets.
And we were glad that the wind is not blowing at us and that we have a place, even, to sit.
So there was my two aunts-- three aunts and my father.
The whole family was there, sitting around.
And we had, already, very little food left, hardly any food.
We dried some challah from home that we took.
And we had just crumbs.
So we gave it to the children, some crumbs and water.
We had the water.
It wasn't clean water.
But we had some water.
And the next day, they said, they have a kitchen there.
So we could go to the kitchen to get some food.
So we always protected my father and my brothers.
Because somehow they were more after the male than the female.
So we went to the kitchen.
And I begged my father and my brothers, they shouldn't go out.
They should stay in the hut.
And so we went out-- the sisters went out to get some food.
So they gave us some soup.
And they also gave us some clean water.
They rationed.
Let's say, for-- for an hour, they would give water.
So we stayed in the line.
And we had some-- we still had some pots.
And we would bring some clean water for drinking.
But it was very little food they gave us there, very little.
And I remember once my brother went-- sneaked out-- and he went near the kitchen.
And he brought us potato peels.
And we washed it, and we ate it, not cooked or nothing, raw.
We ate it as long there was something to eat, anything.
And by then, already, things were getting so bad there.
They used to come at night and kick in the bricks, the Germans.
There we had, already, Germans soldiers.
And they would kick in.
And the-- the hut would fall down.
And we have to build it again.
They-- they played this game, you know?
They enjoyed.
And the kids were screaming.
And-- so we couldn't wash ourselves.
We start getting lice.
And it was very, very bad.
One day, my father passed out.
He fainted.
I don't know if he had a heart attack.
He was so yellow.
And we start screaming.
And somebody came.
She was a nurse in home.
And she checked.
Told my father, she says, maybe he doesn't eat or the condition.
He passed out.
We don't know why.
We started to cry.
We says to my father, because every time we would bring food, he didn't want to eat.
He says, I'm not hungry.
And I used to think to myself, how can he say he's not hungry?
I'm so hungry.
That's all I was think is about food.
How can he not be hungry?
But he didn't want to eat.
He wanted the children should eat.
And that time my sister says-- Lilly-- from now on, daddy, you gonna-- we're gonna share whatever we get.
And you gonna eat your portion.
Because you passed out, because you don't eat.
And we tried he should eat.
But it was just a few days before they took us away from that ghetto.
Right.
They were wearing a different uniform.
The Hungarian soldiers had different uniforms than the-- than the-- the Germans.
I mean, I saw Germans when they occupied the country.
I saw German soldiers, passing by on-- on trucks.
They were wearing different uniforms.
What different?
It was different color.
It was different hats.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was different.
They were also very young, this-- this Germans.
And they had fun hitting Jewish people.
I really feel they used to come into the ghetto.
And they had fun.
They used to take some Jewish boys.
I remember once in particular, this cousin of mine, somehow he stood out from the crowd.
Because, again, he-- they took him.
And we were-- we didn't know where they're going.
And they gave him two bricks in their hand.
And they had to crawl with the two bricks through that dirty, muddy water, all the way across.
And-- and and if they would pick up their head, they would shoot.
They shot a few people already.
They want to have fun by-- by doing all these things.
No.
No.
I didn't see anybody killed.
Well, my father davened in the hut.
Every morning and every night, he would daven.
Yes.
And the boys davened, too.
He still had his talis and tefillin.
And his prayer shawl.
And he would daven, put on every morning, the prayer shawl.
And he-- he prayed every morning and every night.
My uncles, too.
They would pray together.
No.
We didn't have candles to light.
We didn't have-- we had nothing.
I mean, we knew it was shabbos.
The only-- there is things that they knew how-- they prayed the different prayers on shabbos.
But there was nothing observant.
No, no.
There was no Minyan.
They prayed in the hut.
Wherever they stayed, they prayed.
Because they were afraid to get together someplace.
Three weeks.
They-- they came-- came.
The-- they were-- they were there already, like Jewish people that were in charge of organizing things.
So these Jewish people came.
And they said, the people that live in the huts, they have to leave the huts and line up.
They going to the train.
And so we had no choice.
We had to go.
So when we came, like, in the front there, in front of that building, we got together.
It was like a big field where they used to keep the-- the bricks.
But now, there was no more bricks.
Because the people took the bricks away and built huts.
So we came there.
And they said, there were already SS Germans.
And they-- they said we still have some belongings, a blanket, some-- some little things.
And they said, now, you have to throw everything away.
You can't take nothing with you anymore.
We had to go pick up our hand and go through.
They made a-- a line.
The German soldiers made like a-- one side, another.
And you had to go single, by yourself, with your hands up, nothing to carry.
And for some reason, my father saw what's happening.
And he took a little straw basket.
He put in his prayer shawl and his tefillin, which he got it from his father.
And he gave it to me.
[SOBBING] He says, here.
I didn't know what it was in that basket.
He says, you take this.
He says, you could take it.
And he gave it to me.
And I didn't know what it was there.
And I came up front.
And the Nazi told me to-- the German told me to throw it away.
I threw it away.
And I came on the-- [SOBBING] we were running to the train.
And they were hitting, just aiming for the male, hitting.
They had clubs.
And they were hitting everybody.
And people were screaming and running.
[SOBBING] And the train was so high till you could-- it was a cattle car [INAUDIBLE].
We walked up on the train.
So finally, we came up on the train.
And I was walking like in the front.
And my father was in the back of me.
He saw that I threw away the basket.
But he couldn't-- he couldn't go pick it up or something.
And he-- [SOB] then they were aiming to hit him.
And my sister was-- Lilly-- was in the back of him.
So she saw this German want to hit him with a club.
So she stretched out her hand.
And he hit her across the hand, Lilly.
She came up on the-- on the train.
She had-- [SOB] her hand was swollen.
And my father says, I saw you threw away the basket.
I says, yes.
I was scared.
So I says, what was in that basket?
So he says, that was my talis and tefillin.
His prayer shawl that he had it since-- [SOB] since he got married that my mother bought it for him.
[SOBBING] So I says, daddy, why didn't you tell me that what I have in that basket?
I would have give my life to save it?
[CRYING]
He didn't say nothing.
[SOBBING] But I just till this day, I feel so bad that I threw away his prayer shawl and his tefillin.
[CRYING] So Lilly had a swollen hand.
We came up on the train.
They had there-- 10-- 10 breads and a pail.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And we didn't know it was an empty pail.
What in that pail?
What that pail is for?
So we said to my father, we took the breads to share for everybody.
And they were pushing people in, how much they could just push in people.
And it got so crowded.
So we took the 10 breads, and we-- to share the breads.
And I said to my father, how come there's no water?
There's a pail.
Why isn't there any water in that pail?
Because bread and water.
So my father knew right away.
He says, we don't have a bathroom.
He says, this is for the bathroom.
I said, this is for the bathroom?
How can we go in public?
We were raised, we were so religious.
So-- I would never undress.
In front of my sister, I would never get undressed.
And here, you'll have to go to the bathroom in public.
Anyhow, my father says, if you have to go, you'll go.
[SIGHS] It was just-- we were so scared.
We didn't know where we're going or what's happening.
And my sister, Lilly, was-- couldn't pick up her hand.
We thought her hand was broken.
So we-- we [INAUDIBLE]--
Oh, OK.
